Combining: some married club members are students (since all members are students). From statement 3: All married persons → Invited for dance. Therefore, all married club members (who are also married students of the club) are invited for dance — option (b). Option (a) is too strong — not all students are members, and not all members are married. Option (c) is wrong — only 'some' members are married per statement 2, not all. Option (b) is the precise and valid conclusion.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Four political parties W, X, Y and Z decided to set up a joint candidate for the coming parliamentary elections. The formula agreed was the acceptance of a candidate by most of the parties. Four aspiring candidates A, B, C and D approached. A was acceptable to W but not Z. B was acceptable to Y but not X. C was acceptable to W and Y. D was acceptable to W and X. When candidate B was preferred by W and Z, candidate C was preferred by X and Z, and candidate A was acceptable to X but not to Y; who got the ticket?
explanation: Under the modified scenario: B is preferred/accepted by W, Z (newly stated) and Y (original) = W, Y, Z → 3 parties; X does not accept B. A is accepted by W (original) and X (newly stated) but not Y → W, X → 2 parties. C is accepted by W, Y (original) and X, Z (newly preferred) → W, X, Y, Z → 4 parties. D is accepted by W and X → 2 parties. The formula requires acceptance by 'most' parties — i.e., more than half of 4 = at least 3. C is accepted by all 4 parties, B by 3, A by 2, D by 2. C has maximum acceptance and clearly wins. Candidate C gets the ticket.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question:
Consider the following statements:
1. All X-brand cars parked here are white.
2. Some of them have radial tyres.
3. All X-brand cars manufactured after 1986 have radial tyres.
4. All cars are not X-brand.
Which one of the following conclusions can be drawn from the above statements?

option.a: Only white cars are parked here.
option.b: Some white X-brand cars with radial tyres are parked here.
option.c: Cars other than X-brand cannot have radial tyres.
option.d: Most of the X-brand cars are manufactured before 1986.

explanation: From statement 1: All X-brand cars parked here are white. From statement 2: Some X-brand cars (parked here) have radial tyres. Combining: Some white X-brand cars with radial tyres are parked here — option (b) is valid. Option (a) is wrong — statement 4 says not all cars are X-brand, so non-X-brand cars might be parked here and they might not be white. Option (c) is wrong — the passage doesn't restrict radial tyres to X-brand only. Option (d) is wrong — we cannot derive a conclusion about 'most' cars' manufacturing dates from the given statements.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Consider the following statement: 'The Third World War, if it ever starts, will end very quickly with the possible end of civilization. It is only the misuse of nuclear power which will trigger it.' Based on the above statement, which one of the following inferences is correct?

option.a: Nuclear power will be used in the Third World War.
option.b: There will be no civilization left after the Third World War.
option.c: The growth of nuclear power will destroy civilization in the long run.
option.d: The Third World War will not take place.

explanation: The statement says 'it is only the misuse of nuclear power which will trigger it (the Third World War).' This directly implies that if World War III starts, nuclear power will be used/misused as its trigger. Option (a) is a valid inference. Option (b) is too strong — the statement says 'possible end of civilization,' not a certainty. Option (c) goes beyond the statement — it talks about 'the long run' and 'growth of nuclear power,' whereas the statement only addresses WWIII specifically. Option (d) is not inferred — the statement is conditional ('if it ever starts'), not a guarantee that it will not happen.

subject: Quantitative Aptitude
question:
Consider the following information regarding the performance of a class of 1000 students in four different tests:
- Test I: Average 60, Range 30–90
- Test II: Average 60, Range 45–75
- Test III: Average 70, Range 20–100
- Test IV: Average 80, Range 0–100
If a student scores 74 marks in each of the four tests, in which one of the following tests is her performance the best comparatively?
explanation: Compare how far above average the score of 74 is, relative to the range above average: Test I: Above average = 74 − 60 = 14; Max above average = 90 − 60 = 30; Ratio = 14/30 ≈ 0.47. Test II: Above average = 74 − 60 = 14; Max above average = 75 − 60 = 15; Ratio = 14/15 ≈ 0.93. Test III: Above average = 74 − 70 = 4; Max above average = 100 − 70 = 30; Ratio = 4/30 ≈ 0.13. Test IV: 74 < 80 (average), so performance is below average. The highest ratio is in Test II, where 74 is very close to the maximum score of 75 and far above average relative to the range. Thus Test II shows the best comparative performance.

subject: Spatial Reasoning
question: Six squares are coloured, front and back — Red (R), Blue (B), Yellow (Y), Green (G), White (W) and Orange (O) — and are hinged together as shown in the net below. The net arrangement is: [R][B] on top row, [G][Y][O] in middle row, [W] below Y. If they are folded to form a cube, what would be the face opposite to the White face?
explanation: Analysing the net: Y is the center face. B is directly above Y, W is directly below Y, G is to the left of Y, O is to the right of Y. R is to the left of B. When folded into a cube with Y as the base: B folds to become one face (say the front/north face); W, being on the opposite end of the same strip (below Y), folds to become the face directly opposite B. Therefore, W (White) and B (Blue) are opposite faces. The face opposite to White is Blue. R becomes the top/roof face (opposite to Y), G and O are the left-right pair of opposite faces.

subject: Spatial Reasoning
question: In a Venn diagram, circle P represents hardworking people, circle Q represents intelligent people, circle R represents truthful people, and circle S represents honest people. The circles are overlapping with various numbered regions. Which region represents the people who are intelligent, honest and truthful but NOT hardworking?
explanation: In the four-circle Venn diagram: Region 6 represents the intersection of Q (intelligent), R (truthful), and S (honest), but NOT P (hardworking) — i.e., Q ∩ R ∩ S and outside P. Region 7 includes all four circles (P ∩ Q ∩ R ∩ S — hardworking, intelligent, truthful and honest). Region 8 typically includes P ∩ R ∩ S but not Q. Region 11 is typically Q only or Q ∩ P. The people who are intelligent (Q), honest (S), and truthful (R) but not hardworking (not P) fall in region 6, which is the intersection of Q, R and S excluding P.

subject: Spatial Reasoning
question: Three views of a cube following a particular motion are given: View 1 shows K on top, A on left, B on front. View 2 shows H on top, M on left, K on front. View 3 shows B on right, H on top, P on front. What is the letter opposite to A?
explanation: Using the three views to determine adjacency: From View 1, K is adjacent to A, B (both touching K). From View 2, K is adjacent to M, H. So K is adjacent to A, B, M, H — meaning K's opposite = P (the only remaining face). From View 2, H is adjacent to M, K. From View 3, H is adjacent to B, P. So H is adjacent to M, K, B, P — meaning H's opposite = A (the only remaining face). Therefore A and H are opposite faces. The letter opposite to A is H — option (a). This is verified since the 6 letters form 3 opposite pairs: (K, P), (H, A), (B, M).

subject: English Comprehension
question:
Agricultural collectivities such as group-based farming can provide the rural poor:
1. Empowerment.
2. Increased agricultural productivity.
3. Safeguard against exploitative markets.
4. Surplus production of agricultural commodities.
Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
explanation: The passage states: 'the poor especially in market economies, need the strength that collectivities offer for creating more economic, social and political space for themselves' (1-empowerment), 'enhancing agricultural productivity' (2), and 'as a protection against free market individualism' (3-safeguard against exploitative markets). Surplus production (4) is not mentioned as a benefit of agricultural collectivities in the passage. The passage focuses on the social, economic and political benefits of group farming, not on surplus production as an explicit outcome.

subject: English Comprehension
question: What does the author imply by 'gender impact' in the passage on agricultural collectivities?

option.a: Women are doubtful participants in cooperatives.
option.b: Family cooperatives may not include women.
option.d: Women's role in transition economies is highly restrictive.

explanation: The passage states: 'although the gender impact of the family cooperatives in the transition economies are uncertain, the Indian examples of women-only groups farming offer considerable potential for benefiting women.' The phrase 'gender impact...are uncertain' in the context of family cooperatives implies that family cooperatives may not fully include or benefit women — option (b). The author suggests women-only groups as an alternative precisely because the gender impact of family cooperatives is uncertain. Option (c) would be too positive a reading, and option (d) is too definitive a statement not supported by the text.

subject: English Comprehension
question:
Consider the following assumptions with reference to the passage on agricultural collectivities:
1. It is imperative for transition economies to have agricultural collectivities.
2. Agricultural productivity can be increased by group approach to farming.
Which of these assumptions is/are valid?
explanation: Assumption 2 is valid: The passage directly states 'group approach to farming...offers substantial scope for poverty alleviation and empowering the poor as well as enhancing agricultural productivity.' This confirms that group farming can increase agricultural productivity. Assumption 1 is not valid: The passage does not say agricultural collectivities are 'imperative' for transition economies. It merely says there are 'many notable examples of such collectivities to be found in varied contexts, such as in the transition economies.' The existence of examples does not make them mandatory or imperative. The passage presents collectivities as beneficial, not obligatory.

subject: English Comprehension
subtopic: Democracy in India — Western vs Indian Context
question: According to the passage, what does 'deepening of democracy' mean in the Western context?

option.a: Consolidation of group and class identities.
option.b: Democratization translated as greater involvement of people.
option.c: Democratization as greater involvement of 'atomized' individuals in the public sphere.
option.d: None of the statements (a), (b) and (c) given above is correct in this context.

explanation: The passage explicitly states: 'In a typical Western liberal context, deepening of democracy invariably leads to consolidation of liberal values. In the Indian context, democratization is translated into greater involvement of people not as individuals — which is a staple to liberal discourse — but as communities or groups.' The key contrast is that in Western liberal tradition, individuals are treated as 'atomized' persons. In the Western context, deepening democracy means greater involvement of atomized individuals in the public sphere. Option (a) and (b) mix up the Indian and Western contexts. Option (c) correctly captures the Western liberal understanding.

subject: English Comprehension
question: Greater democratization in India has NOT necessarily led to:

option.a: The dilution of caste and communal identities in the public sphere.
option.b: Irrelevance of community identity as a governing force in Indian politics.
option.c: Marginalization of elite groups in society.
option.d: Relative unimportance of hereditary identities over class identities.

explanation: The passage states that in India, 'individuals are getting involved in the public sphere not as atomized individuals but as members of primordial communities drawn on religious or caste identity. Community-identity seems to be the governing force.' The so-called peripheral groups 'continue to maintain their identities with reference to the social groups (caste, religion or sect).' This means democratization in India has NOT led to the dilution of caste and communal identities — rather, these identities persist and even strengthen. Option (a) correctly identifies what has NOT happened. The passage also explicitly says community identity remains a 'governing force' — contradicting options (b) as well.

subject: English Comprehension
question: What is the 'silent revolution' that has occurred in the Indian democratic process?

option.a: Irrelevance of caste and class hierarchies in political processes.
option.b: Loosening of social strictures in voting behaviour and patterns.
option.c: Social change through transfer of power from upper caste elites to subaltern groups.
option.d: All the statements (a), (b) and (c) given above are correct in this context.

explanation: The passage explicitly defines the 'silent revolution': 'This is a significant political process that had led to a silent revolution through a meaningful transfer of power from the upper caste elites to various subaltern groups within the democratic framework of public governance.' This perfectly matches option (c). Option (a) is wrong — caste hierarchies have NOT become irrelevant; the passage says caste identity remains a governing force. Option (b) is partially reflected in the passage ('loosening of social strictures') but not the core definition of the silent revolution. Option (c) is the direct, textual definition provided in the passage.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Guest lectures on five subjects — Economics, History, Statistics, English and Mathematics — have to be arranged from Monday to Friday (one per day). Economics cannot be scheduled on Tuesday. History is available only on Tuesday. Mathematics must be scheduled immediately after the day of Economics lecture. English must be scheduled immediately before the day of Economics lecture. Which lecture is scheduled on Monday?
explanation: Working out the schedule: History = Tuesday (only day available). The sequence must be English → Economics → Mathematics on consecutive days. Economics ≠ Tuesday. If Economics = Thursday: English = Wednesday, Mathematics = Friday. Monday = Statistics. If Economics = Wednesday: English = Tuesday, but Tuesday = History (conflict). If Economics = Friday: Mathematics = Saturday (impossible). Therefore: Monday = Statistics, Tuesday = History, Wednesday = English, Thursday = Economics, Friday = Mathematics. Monday's lecture is Statistics.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Using the same lecture scheduling problem (Economics cannot be on Tuesday; History only on Tuesday; Mathematics immediately after Economics; English immediately before Economics): Which lecture is scheduled between Statistics and English?
explanation: From the derived schedule: Monday = Statistics, Tuesday = History, Wednesday = English, Thursday = Economics, Friday = Mathematics. The lecture between Statistics (Monday) and English (Wednesday) is on Tuesday — which is History. Therefore, History is scheduled between Statistics and English. This also confirms that the sequence Statistics → History → English is consecutive Monday to Wednesday.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Using the same lecture scheduling problem: Which lecture is scheduled on Wednesday?
explanation: From the derived schedule: Monday = Statistics, Tuesday = History, Wednesday = English, Thursday = Economics, Friday = Mathematics. Wednesday's lecture is English. This follows because English must be immediately before Economics (Thursday), and the only day immediately before Thursday is Wednesday. The complete validated schedule is: Mon-Statistics, Tue-History, Wed-English, Thu-Economics, Fri-Mathematics.

subject: Quantitative Aptitude
question: Two glasses of equal volume are respectively half and three-fourths filled with milk. They are then filled to the brim by adding water. Their contents are then poured into another vessel. What will be the ratio of milk to water in this vessel?
explanation: Let the volume of each glass = $V$. Glass 1: Milk = $\frac{V}{2}$, Water added = $\frac{V}{2}$. Glass 2: Milk = $\frac{3V}{4}$, Water added = $\frac{V}{4}$. After combining: Total Milk = $\frac{V}{2} + \frac{3V}{4} = \frac{2V + 3V}{4} = \frac{5V}{4}$. Total Water = $\frac{V}{2} + \frac{V}{4} = \frac{2V + V}{4} = \frac{3V}{4}$. Ratio Milk : Water = $\frac{5V}{4} : \frac{3V}{4} = 5 : 3$.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question:
Examine the following statements:
1. None but the rich can afford air-travel.
2. Some of those who travel by air become sick.
3. Some of those who become sick require treatment.
Which one of the following conclusions can be drawn from the above statements?

option.a: All the rich persons travel by air.
option.b: Those who travel by air become sick.
option.c: All the rich persons become sick.
option.d: All those who travel by air are rich.

explanation: Statement 1 says 'None but the rich can afford air-travel,' meaning only rich people can travel by air. This is equivalent to: 'If someone travels by air → they are rich.' Option (d) 'All those who travel by air are rich' directly follows from this. Option (a) is wrong — statement 1 says only rich can afford it, not that all rich people do travel by air. Option (b) is wrong — only 'some' of those who travel by air become sick, not all. Option (c) cannot be derived — not all rich people travel by air, and even those who do, only 'some' become sick.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: In five flats, one above the other, live five professionals: Doctor, Professor, IAS officer, Engineer, MLA. The Professor has to go up to meet his IAS officer friend. The Doctor is equally friendly to all, and has to go up as frequently as go down. The Engineer has to go up to meet his MLA friend above whose flat lives the Professor's friend (IAS officer). From the ground floor to the top floor, in what order do the five professionals live?

option.a: Engineer, Professor, Doctor, IAS officer, MLA
option.b: Professor, Engineer, Doctor, IAS officer, MLA
option.c: IAS officer, Engineer, Doctor, Professor, MLA
option.d: Professor, Engineer, Doctor, MLA, IAS officer

explanation: Analysing the constraints: (1) Professor goes UP to IAS officer → IAS is above Professor. (2) Engineer goes UP to MLA → MLA is above Engineer. (3) MLA's flat is above the Engineer's, and above that MLA flat lives the Professor's friend (IAS officer) → IAS is above MLA, which is above Engineer. (4) Professor is above someone (goes up to IAS). (5) Doctor goes up as frequently as down → Doctor is in the middle position (3rd floor). From constraint (3): Engineer < MLA < IAS. From constraint (1): Professor < IAS. With Doctor in 3rd position: Professor(1st) → Engineer(2nd) → Doctor(3rd) → IAS(4th) → MLA(5th)... but IAS must be above MLA from constraint (3). Re-checking: Professor(1) < IAS somewhere above; Engineer(2) < MLA < IAS. So order: Professor(1), Engineer(2), Doctor(3), IAS(4), MLA(5) doesn't satisfy IAS > MLA. Correct order: Professor(1), Engineer(2), Doctor(3), IAS(4), MLA(5) with IAS officer above Engineer but MLA at top — the passage says 'MLA friend above whose flat lives the Professor's friend (IAS officer)' meaning IAS is above MLA? Or MLA is above IAS? Reading again: 'The Engineer has to go up to meet his MLA friend above whose flat lives the Professor's friend.' 'Above whose flat' = above MLA's flat lives IAS. So IAS > MLA > Engineer. Order from bottom: Professor, Engineer, Doctor, MLA, IAS. But that matches option (d). However, if Professor goes up to IAS, Professor < IAS ✓. Option (b) has IAS at 4th and MLA at 5th (MLA > IAS), while option (d) has MLA at 4th and IAS at 5th (IAS > MLA). Based on the constraint that IAS is above MLA, option (d) is correct. Answer: (d).

subject: English Comprehension
question: Which of the following best explains the sentence in the passage: 'I was almost a part of it'?

option.a: I was not alone in the cell.
option.b: I was familiar with every bit of the cell.
option.c: I greeted little tufts of grass like old friends.
option.d: I felt quite at home in the cell.

explanation: The phrase 'I was almost a part of it' refers to the narrator's deep familiarity and sense of belonging in the prison cell after fourteen and a half months. The author knew 'every mark and dent on the whitewashed walls and on the uneven floor,' greeted grass and stones as 'old friends,' and co-existed with wasps, hornets, and lizards. This total integration with the environment suggests the narrator felt at home — as if they had merged with the cell itself. Option (d) best captures this sense of being at home. Options (a), (b), and (c) each capture partial aspects but not the full emotional meaning of 'almost a part of it.'

subject: English Comprehension
question: The passage about the prison cell attempts to describe:

option.a: The general conditions of the country's jails.
option.b: The prisoner's capacity to notice the minute details of his surroundings.
option.c: The prisoner's conscious efforts to overcome the loneliness.
option.d: The prisoner's ability to live happily with other creatures.

explanation: The passage meticulously details the narrator's awareness of 'every mark and dent on the whitewashed walls and on the uneven floor and the ceiling with its moth-eaten rafters,' the 'little tufts of grass and odd bits of stone' in the yard, and the colonies of wasps, hornets, and lizards. This intense awareness and cataloguing of minute details is the primary focus of the passage. Option (a) is too general — the passage is personal, not a survey of jail conditions. Option (c) is not explicitly stated — there's no mention of conscious effort to overcome loneliness. Option (d) is partially true but secondary to the main theme of hyperawareness of surroundings.

subject: English Comprehension
question: What does the expression 'pitching the highest camp' imply in the passage about mountain climbing?

option.a: They reached the summit of the highest mountain in the world.
option.b: Those who climbed that far earlier did not pitch any camp.
option.c: So far nobody has ever climbed that high.
option.d: They were too many climbers and needed to pitch a big camp.

explanation: The passage begins: 'We started pitching the highest camp that has ever been made.' The phrase 'that has ever been made' directly implies that no one before had set up a camp at that altitude. This is not about reaching a summit — they are still setting up camp. Option (c) 'So far nobody has ever climbed that high' is the correct implication — they are breaking an altitude record. Option (a) is wrong — they are pitching camp, not summiting. Option (b) is incorrect — others had climbed high but not pitched camp at that elevation. Option (d) is irrelevant to the text.

subject: English Comprehension
question: In the mountain climbing passage, they took a long time to finish the work because:

option.b: There was not enough air to breathe.

explanation: The passage explicitly states: 'Everything took five times as long as it would have taken in a place where there was enough air to breathe.' This directly tells us the primary reason for slowness was insufficient oxygen at that extreme altitude — not fatigue, cold, or darkness. Option (a) is a possible secondary factor but not stated as the reason. Option (c) is also not stated as the cause of delay — the tent was described as 'not too cold' inside. Option (d) is not mentioned at all. The text gives a clear, direct cause: lack of sufficient air to breathe at that altitude.

subject: English Comprehension
question: When they crawled into the tent during the mountain climbing expedition:

option.a: They took off their gloves because it was not very cold.
option.b: They could not take off their gloves because it was very cold.
option.c: They took off their gloves though it was very cold.
option.d: They did not take off their gloves though it was not cold.

explanation: The passage says: 'inside it was not too cold for us to take off our gloves.' The double negative is key — 'not too cold' means it was cold, but not excessively so. The result was that they DID take off their gloves — it was cold (high altitude) but cold enough that they COULD remove gloves without extreme discomfort. Option (a) is wrong — it was cold, just not 'too cold.' Option (b) is wrong — they were able to and did take off their gloves. Option (d) is wrong — it WAS cold. Option (c) correctly captures that they removed gloves despite the cold conditions.

subject: English Comprehension
question: In the fire passage, the man went into another room because:

option.a: He did not know where exactly the stairway was.
option.b: The passage was full of smoke.

explanation: The passage states: 'Losing his way in a smoke-filled passage, he missed the stairway and went into another room.' The reason is explicit — the smoke-filled passage disoriented him, causing him to miss the stairway. Option (a) is a consequence of the smoke, not the primary cause — the smoke caused him to lose his way, not a lack of general knowledge about the stairway location. Option (c) nervousness is not mentioned. Option (d) he stumbled on the bundle after entering the room, which is a consequence, not the cause of entering the room.

subject: English Comprehension
question: In the fire passage, the man was called a hero because he:

option.a: Expressed his willingness to risk his life to save others.
option.b: Managed to escape from the fire.
option.c: Showed great courage in fighting the fire.

explanation: The passage recounts that the man 'picked up a bundle to protect his face from the fire and immediately fell through the floor below where he managed to escape through a clear doorway.' The 'bundle' turned out to be the baby of the Mayor's wife, and the 'hero was congratulated by all.' He saved the baby's life — option (d). He didn't explicitly express willingness to save others (a), nor did he fight the fire (c). He escaped (b) but that alone wouldn't make him a hero. The heroic act was the accidental/fortunate saving of the baby's life.

subject: Decision Making
question: You have differences of opinion regarding the final report prepared by your subordinate that is to be submitted urgently. The subordinate is justifying the information given in the report. You would...

option.a: Convince the subordinate that he is wrong.
option.b: Tell him to reconsider the results.
option.c: Revise the report on your own.
option.d: Tell him not to justify the mistake.

explanation: The most professional and balanced approach in this situation is to ask the subordinate to reconsider the results — option (b). This respects the subordinate's input while also exercising supervisory responsibility to ensure accuracy. Option (a) 'convince the subordinate he is wrong' is presumptuous — you may yourself be wrong in the difference of opinion. Option (c) revising the report unilaterally undermines the subordinate and bypasses collaboration. Option (d) telling him not to justify is autocratic and shuts down legitimate professional dialogue. Asking for reconsideration is constructive, respectful, and appropriate for an urgent situation.

subject: Decision Making
question: You are competing with your batch-mate for a prestigious award to be decided based on an oral presentation. Ten minutes are allowed for each presentation. You have been asked by the committee to finish on time. Your friend, however, is allowed more than the stipulated time period. You would...

option.a: Lodge a complaint to the chairperson against the discrimination.
option.b: Not listen to any justification from the committee.
option.c: Ask for withdrawal of your name.

explanation: The most appropriate response is to formally raise the issue through proper channels — lodge a complaint with the chairperson (option a). This is the ethical and procedural way to address discrimination while remaining within the system. Option (b) refusing to listen to justification is unproductive and confrontational — there may be a valid reason you are unaware of. Option (c) withdrawing your name is an extreme overreaction and forfeits your legitimate opportunity. Option (d) protesting and leaving is disruptive and unprofessional. Raising a formal complaint with the chairperson ensures due process and protects your rights.

subject: Decision Making
question: You are handling a time-bound project. During the project review meeting, you find that the project is likely to get delayed due to lack of cooperation of the team members. You would...

option.a: Warn the team members for their non-cooperation.
option.c: Ask for the replacement of team members.
option.d: Ask for extension of time citing reasons.

explanation: The most effective and leadership-oriented approach is to first understand why the team is not cooperating — option (b). Non-cooperation rarely occurs without reason; there may be unclear instructions, resource shortfalls, interpersonal conflicts, or personal issues. Diagnosing the root cause is the first step to fixing it. Option (a) warning team members may escalate tensions without resolving the underlying problem. Option (c) asking for replacements is extreme and disruptive, especially in a time-bound project. Option (d) seeking an extension without first trying to resolve the issue is avoidance. Understanding the problem comes first.

subject: Decision Making
question: As a citizen you have some work with a government department. The official calls you again and again, and without directly asking you, sends out feelers for a bribe. You want to get your work done. You would...

option.b: Behave as if you have not understood the feelers and persist with your application.
option.c: Go to the higher officer for help verbally complaining about feelers.

explanation: The most appropriate ethical response is to send a formal complaint — option (d). This creates an official record, puts the concerned official on notice, and uses the proper anti-corruption mechanism. Option (a) giving a bribe is illegal, unethical, and perpetuates corruption. Option (b) pretending not to understand feelers may temporarily delay but doesn't resolve the situation and doesn't address the corrupt behavior. Option (c) verbally complaining to a higher officer is better than giving a bribe, but lacks the formal record that protects the complainant and creates accountability. A formal written complaint is the most effective and principled approach.

subject: CSAT
question: What do you understand by the instrumentalist view of education?

option.a: Education is functional and utilitarian in its purposes.
option.b: Education is meant to fulfil human needs.
option.c: The purpose of education is to train the human intellect.
option.d: Education is meant to achieve moral development.

explanation: The instrumentalist view of education sees education as a tool or instrument for achieving practical outcomes such as economic productivity, job skills, and societal needs. It emphasizes functional and utilitarian purposes rather than intrinsic intellectual or moral development. Option (a) directly captures this utilitarian perspective, while options (c) and (d) focus on intrinsic intellectual or moral goals, which are not central to instrumentalism. Option (b) is broader and less specific to the instrumentalist philosophy. Therefore, (a) is correct.

subject: CSAT
question: According to the passage, education must be respected in itself because

option.a: it helps to acquire qualifications for employment.
option.b: it helps in upward mobility and acquiring social status.
option.c: it is an inner process of moral and intellectual development.
option.d: All the (a), (b) and (c) given above are correct in this context.

explanation: The question refers to a missing passage, but based on typical UPSC themes, education is respected for its intrinsic value in moral and intellectual development, not merely for external benefits like jobs or status. Option (c) reflects the inherent worth of education as a transformative inner process. Options (a) and (b) are extrinsic motivations, which are not the primary reason to respect education in itself. Thus, (c) is the correct answer.

subject: CSAT
question: Education is a process in which

option.a: students are converted into trained professionals.
option.b: opportunities for higher income are generated.
option.c: individuals develop self-critical awareness and independence of thought.
option.d: qualifications for upward mobility are acquired.

explanation: Education as a process focuses on holistic development, including critical thinking and intellectual independence. Option (c) captures the essence of true education beyond vocational training or income generation. Options (a), (b), and (d) are instrumental outcomes but not the core process of education itself. Hence, (c) is correct.

subject: CSAT
question: "The evolution of pesticide resistance is natural selection in action." What does it actually imply?

option.a: It is very natural for many organisms to have pesticide resistance.
option.b: Pesticide resistance among organisms is a universal phenomenon.
option.c: Some individuals in any given population show resistance after the application of pesticides.
option.d: None of the statements (a), (b) and (c) given above is correct.

explanation: The statement means that natural selection acts on existing genetic variation: when pesticides are applied, resistant individuals survive and reproduce. Option (c) correctly states that some individuals in a population exhibit resistance after pesticide application. Option (a) is incorrect because resistance is not 'natural' in the sense of being innate to all organisms; it arises from selection. Option (b) is false because resistance is not universal; many populations lack resistance. Therefore, (c) is correct.

subject: CSAT
question: With reference to the passage, consider the following statements: 1. Use of chemical pesticides has become imperative in all the poor countries of the world. 2. Chemical pesticides should not have any role in sustainable agriculture. 3. One pest can develop resistance to many pesticides. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: From the passage: Statement 1 is false because the passage says in poorer countries pesticides are used due to threats of starvation or disease, but not 'imperative in all' - it's context-dependent. Statement 2 is false because the passage acknowledges that pesticide use can be sustainable if new pesticides are developed. Statement 3 is true: the passage gives the example of the Alabama leafworm developing resistance to multiple pesticides (aldrin, DDT, etc.). Hence only statement 3 is correct, so answer (b).

subject: CSAT
question: Though the problems associated with the use of chemical pesticides is known for a long time, their widespread use has not waned. Why?

option.a: Alternatives to chemical pesticides do not exist at all.
option.b: New pesticides are not invented at all.
option.d: None of the statements (a), (b) and (c) given above is correct.

explanation: The passage explains that pesticide use continues because the cost-benefit ratio favors their use (e.g., $5 benefit per $1 spent), and in poor countries they help prevent starvation and disease. Options (a), (b), and (c) are false: alternatives exist (though not widespread), new pesticides are invented, and while some are biodegradable, that's not the reason for continued use. Thus none of the given statements explain why use hasn't waned, so (d) is correct.

subject: CSAT
question: How do pesticides act as agents for the selection of resistant individuals in any pest population? 1. It is possible that in a pest population the individuals will behave differently due to their genetic makeup. 2. Pests do possess the ability to detoxify the pesticides. 3. Evolution of pesticide resistance is equally distributed in pest population. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Statement 1 is correct: genetic variation causes individuals to behave differently (e.g., resistant vs. susceptible). Statement 2 is correct: some pests possess detoxifying enzymes, as mentioned in the passage. Statement 3 is false: resistance evolution is not equally distributed; it varies within and among populations. Therefore, statements 1 and 2 are correct, so answer (b).

subject: CSAT
question: Why is the use of chemical pesticides generally justified by giving the examples of poor and developing countries? 1. Developed countries can afford to do away with use of pesticides by adapting to organic farming, but it is imperative for poor and developing countries to use chemical pesticides. 2. In poor and developing countries, the pesticide addresses the problem of epidemic diseases of crops and eases the food problem. 3. The social and health costs of pesticide use are generally ignored in poor and developing countries. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The passage states that in poorer countries, the threat of mass starvation or epidemic disease leads to ignoring social/health costs, and pesticides ease food problems. Statement 2 directly reflects this. Statement 1 is incorrect because the passage does not claim developed countries can afford organic farming; it says pesticide use is justified in poor countries due to immediate threats. Statement 3 is mentioned but as a consequence, not a justification; the justification is saving lives and food production. However, the question asks 'generally justified by giving examples' - the passage cites 'lives saved' and 'food produced' as justifications, not the ignoring of costs. So only statement 2 is directly correct. Hence answer (c).

subject: CSAT
question: What does the passage imply?

option.a: Alternative options to chemical pesticides should be promoted.
option.b: Too much use of chemicals is not good for the ecosystem.
option.c: There is no scope for the improvement of pesticides and making their use sustainable.
option.d: Both the statements (a) and (b) above are correct.

explanation: The passage discusses the problems of pesticide resistance but also notes that sustainability depends on developing new, less persistent, biodegradable pesticides. It implies that alternatives should be promoted (a) and that excessive chemical use harms ecosystems (b). Option (c) is false because the passage sees scope for improvement. Therefore both (a) and (b) are implied, so (d) is correct.

subject: CSAT
question: Which of the following conditions of growth can add to vulnerability? 1. When the growth occurs due to excessive exploitation of mineral resources and forests. 2. When the growth brings about a change in humankind's creative potential. 3. When the growth is envisaged only for providing houses and social security to the people. 4. When the growth occurs due to emphasis on farming only. Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
explanation: From Passage-3: The passage states that growth strategies can add to vulnerability if they overexploit natural resources (e.g., irrigated cotton in Central Asia leading to Aral Sea disappearance, and clearing mangroves). Statement 1 matches this. Statement 2: creative potential is actually needed for adaptation, not a vulnerability. Statement 3: providing houses and social security reduces vulnerability, not adds. Statement 4: emphasis on farming alone is not inherently vulnerable; it depends on practices. Only statement 1 is correct, so answer (a).

subject: CSAT
question: What does low-carbon growth imply in the present context? 1. More emphasis on the use of renewable sources of energy. 2. Less emphasis on manufacturing sector and more emphasis on agriculture sector. 3. Switching over from monoculture practices to mixed farming. 4. Less demand for goods and services. Select the correct answer using the codes given below:

option.d: None of the above implies low-carbon growth

explanation: Low-carbon growth focuses on reducing carbon emissions, primarily through renewable energy (statement 1). Statements 2 and 3 relate to agriculture but not necessarily low-carbon; manufacturing can be low-carbon. Statement 4 (less demand) is not implied; growth can occur with low carbon. Thus only statement 1 is correct, so answer (a).

subject: CSAT
question: Which of the following conditions is/are necessary for sustainable economic growth? 1. Spreading of economic prosperity more. 2. Popularising/spreading of adaptive technologies widely. 3. Investing on research in adaptation and mitigation technologies. Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
explanation: The passage emphasizes that sustaining growth requires greater capacity to understand environment, generate adaptive technologies, and diffuse them widely (statements 2 and 3). Also, spreading economic prosperity helps adaptation (statement 1). All three are necessary according to the passage, so (d) is correct.

subject: CSAT
question: Which of the following inferences can be made from the passage? 1. Rainfed crops should not be cultivated in irrigated areas. 2. Farming under water-deficient areas should not be a part of development strategy. Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
explanation: The passage gives an example of irrigated cotton cultivation in water-stressed Central Asia leading to environmental disaster, but it does not explicitly say rainfed crops shouldn't be in irrigated areas (1) or that water-deficient farming shouldn't be part of development (2). The passage warns against overexploitation, not against farming per se. Thus neither inference is directly supported, so (d) is correct.

subject: CSAT
question: Consider the following assumptions: 1. Sustainable economic growth demands the use of creative potential of man. 2. Intensive agriculture can lead to ecological backlash. 3. Spread of economic prosperity can adversely affect the ecology and environment. With reference to the passage, which of the above assumptions is/are valid?
explanation: The passage states that creative potential is directed at adaptation (assumption 1 valid). Intensive agriculture example (Aral Sea) shows ecological backlash (2 valid). Spread of prosperity can lead to overexploitation and vulnerability (3 valid). All three are supported, so (d).

subject: CSAT
question: Which one of the following statements constitutes the central theme of this passage?

option.a: Countries with greater economic prosperity are better equipped to deal with the consequences of climate change.
option.b: Adaptation and mitigation should be integrated with development strategies.
option.c: Rapid economic growth should not be pursued by both developed and developing economies.
option.d: Some countries resort to overexploitation of natural resources for the sake of rapid development.

explanation: The passage's central theme is that adaptation and mitigation need to be integrated into a climate-smart development strategy. Option (b) directly captures this. Option (a) is a supporting point but not central. Option (c) is not stated; growth is not condemned. Option (d) is an example, not the main theme. Hence (b) is correct.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Consider the following statements: 1. All artists are whimsical. 2. Some artists are drug addicts. 3. Frustrated people are prone to become drug addicts. From the above three statements it may be concluded that

option.c: All frustrated people are drug addicts.

explanation: From statement 1, all artists are whimsical. Statement 2 says some artists are drug addicts. Therefore, those artists who are drug addicts are also whimsical (since all artists are whimsical). Hence some drug addicts are whimsical. Option (b) follows. Option (a) is not supported; no link between artists and frustration. Option (c) reverses statement 3. Option (d) is a generalization not supported. So (b) is correct.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Examine the following statements: 1. Either A and B are of the same age or A is older than B. 2. Either C and D are of the same age or D is older than C. 3. B is older than C. Which one of the following conclusions can be drawn from the above statements?

option.b: B and D are of the same age

explanation: Statement 1: A ≥ B. Statement 3: B > C. Combining, A ≥ B > C, so A > C. Thus A is older than C. Option (d) follows. Option (a) is not necessarily true because A could equal B. Option (b) cannot be determined. Option (c) is already given in statement 2 as possibility but not necessarily true (C and D could be same age). Hence (d) is correct.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Examine the following statements: 1. Only those who have a pair of binoculars can become the members of the birdwatcher's club. 2. Some members of the birdwatcher's club have cameras. 3. Those members who have cameras can take part in photo-contests. Which of the following conclusions can be drawn from the above statements?

option.a: All those who have a pair of binoculars are members of the birdwatcher's club.
option.b: All members of the birdwatcher's club have a pair of binoculars.
option.c: All those who take part in photo-contests are members of the birdwatcher's club.

explanation: Statement 1: 'Only those who have binoculars can become members' means having binoculars is a necessary condition. So all members have binoculars. Option (b) is correct. Option (a) is the converse, not necessarily true. Option (c) is not supported; photo-contest participants may include non-members. Therefore (b) follows.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: During the last summer vacation, Ankit went to a summer camp where he took part in hiking, swimming and boating. This summer, he is looking forward to a music camp where he hopes to sing, dance and learn to play the guitar. Based on the above information, four conclusions, as given below, have been made. Which one of these logically follows from the information given above?

option.a: Ankit's parents want him to play the guitar.
option.b: Ankit prefers music to outdoor activities.
option.c: Ankit goes to some type of camp every summer.
option.d: Ankit likes to sing and dance.

explanation: The passage states that Ankit hopes to sing, dance, and learn guitar at the music camp. This directly implies he likes to sing and dance (option d). Option (a) is not mentioned about parents. Option (b) is not stated; he may enjoy both. Option (c) is not supported because only two summers are mentioned, not every summer. Thus (d) follows.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Three persons A, B and C wore shirts of black, blue and orange colours (not necessarily in that order) and pants of green, yellow and orange colours (not necessarily in that order). No person wore shirt and pants of the same colour. Further, it is given that 1. A did not wear shirt of black colour. 2. B did not wear shirt of blue colour. 3. C did not wear shirt of orange colour. 4. A did not wear pants of green colour. 5. B wore pants of orange colour. What were the colours of the pants and shirt worn by C, respectively?
explanation: Solved using logic: From (5) B pants orange. B shirt not blue (2) and cannot be orange (same color), so B shirt black. C shirt not orange (3), so C shirt blue (only left). Then A shirt orange. Pants: B orange; A not green (4), so A pants yellow; C pants green. Thus C wears green pants and blue shirt. Option (b) matches.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Ten new TV shows started in January - 5 sitcoms, 3 drama and 2 news magazines. By April, only seven of the new shows were still on, five of them being sitcoms. Based on the above information, four conclusions, as given below, have been made. Which one of these logically follows from the information given above?

option.a: Only one news magazine show is still on.
option.b: Only one of the drama shows is still on.
option.c: At least one discontinued show was a drama.

explanation: Initially: 5 sitcoms, 3 dramas, 2 news. By April: 7 shows remain, of which 5 are sitcoms. So remaining 2 shows are from drama and news. Total dramas (3) and news (2) = 5 shows. Since only 2 of these 5 remain, at least 3 were discontinued. Among dramas (3 total), it's possible all 3 discontinued? But then 2 news would have to remain? However only 2 non-sitcom remain, so if all 3 dramas discontinued, then both news remain (2) - that works. So it's possible no drama remains? Actually if both news remain (2), then dramas all 3 discontinued. That means at least one drama was discontinued (actually all three). So statement (c) 'at least one discontinued show was a drama' is necessarily true because even if all dramas discontinued, that satisfies. Could it be that no drama was discontinued? That would mean all 3 dramas remain, but then only 2 non-sitcom slots, impossible because 3 > 2. So at least one drama must have been discontinued. Thus (c) follows. (a) and (b) are not necessarily true; could be 2 news or 2 dramas remaining. (d) is opinion not supported. So (c).

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Read the passage given below and the two statements that follow (given on the basis of the passage): Four men are waiting at Delhi airport for a Mumbai flight. Two are doctors and the other two are businessmen. Two speak Gujarati and two speak Tamil. No two of the same profession speak the same language. Two are Muslims and two are Christians. No two of the same religion are of the same profession, nor do they speak the same language. The Tamil-speaking doctor is a Christian. 1. The Christian-businessman speaks Gujarati. 2. The Gujarati-speaking doctor is a Muslim. Which of the above statements is/are correct conclusion/conclusions?
explanation: Let's denote: Persons: D1, D2 (doctors), B1, B2 (businessmen). Languages: Guj, Tamil. Religions: Muslim, Christian. Conditions: No same profession same language; no same religion same profession; no same religion same language. Given: Tamil-speaking doctor is Christian. So that doctor (say D1) is Tamil and Christian. Then the other doctor (D2) cannot be Tamil (since no same profession same language) and cannot be Christian (since no same religion same profession), so D2 must be Gujarati and Muslim. Now businessmen: The two businessmen must have languages: one Tamil, one Gujarati (since both languages used). Also religions: one Muslim, one Christian (since two each). But no same religion same language: The Christian businessman cannot speak Tamil because Christian already speaks Tamil (D1) and no same religion same language? Actually condition: 'No two of the same religion ... speak the same language.' So the Christian businessman cannot speak Tamil (since D1 Christian speaks Tamil). So Christian businessman must speak Gujarati. That matches statement 1. Also the Muslim businessman then speaks Tamil. Now statement 2: The Gujarati-speaking doctor is a Muslim - yes, D2 is Gujarati and Muslim. So both statements are correct. Hence (c).

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Consider the following statement: 'Though quite expensive, television is not a luxury item, as one can learn many things through television.' Which one of the following is a valid inference from the above statement?

option.a: All expensive things are regarded as luxury.
option.b: All essential things for learning are not luxury.
option.d: Television is not a luxury item.

explanation: The statement directly concludes that television is not a luxury item, despite being expensive, because it has learning value. Option (d) restates the conclusion. Option (a) is false; the statement contradicts that. Option (b) is too broad and not stated. Option (c) says 'essential' which is stronger than 'can learn many things'. Hence (d) is the valid inference.

subject: Quantitative Aptitude
question: Mr. Kumar drives to work at an average speed of 48 km per hour. The time taken to cover the first 60% of the distance is 10 minutes more than the time taken to cover the remaining distance. How far is his office?
explanation: Let total distance = D km. First 60% = 0.6D, remaining = 0.4D. Time = distance/speed. Speed constant 48 km/hr. Time1 = 0.6D/48 hours, Time2 = 0.4D/48 hours. Given Time1 - Time2 = 10 minutes = 10/60 = 1/6 hour. So (0.6D/48) - (0.4D/48) = 1/6 => (0.2D)/48 = 1/6 => 0.2D = 48/6 = 8 => D = 8 / 0.2 = 40 km. Thus answer (b).

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Gita is prettier than Sita but not as pretty as Rita. Then,

option.a: Sita is not as pretty as Gita.
option.c: Rita is not as pretty as Gita.

explanation: Given: Gita > Sita (prettier), and Rita > Gita (since Gita not as pretty as Rita). So order: Rita > Gita > Sita. Option (a) says Sita is not as pretty as Gita, which is true (Gita > Sita). Option (b) false; (c) false; (d) false. Thus (a) is correct.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Given that, 1. A is the brother of B. 2. C is the father of A. 3. D is the brother of E. 4. E is the daughter of B. Then, the uncle of D is
explanation: From (1) A and B are siblings (A brother of B). From (2) C is father of A, so also father of B. From (4) E is daughter of B, so B is parent of E. From (3) D is brother of E, so D and E are siblings, thus D is also child of B. Therefore D's uncle would be brother of B (since uncle is parent's brother). B's brother is A. So uncle of D is A. Option (a) correct.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Examine the following statements: 1. Rama scored more than Rani. 2. Rani scored less than Ratna. 3. Ratna scored more than Rama. 4. Padma scored more than Rama but less than Ratna. Who scored the highest?
explanation: From (1) Rama > Rani. (2) Rani < Ratna means Ratna > Rani. (3) Ratna > Rama. (4) Padma > Rama and Padma < Ratna. So combining: Ratna > Padma > Rama > Rani. Thus Ratna scored highest. Answer (d).

subject: Reading Comprehension
question: With reference to the passage, which of the following statements is correct?

option.a: Introduction of exotic species into new geographical areas always leads to reduced biodiversity.
option.b: Exotic species introduced by man into new areas have always greatly altered the native ecosystems.
option.c: Man is the only reason to convert a hugely diverse range of local community compositions into more homogeneous ones.
option.d: None of the statements (a), (b) and (c) is correct in this context.

explanation: The passage states that not all introductions lead to reduced biodiversity (e.g., introducing European species to Britain could augment biodiversity). Also, some introductions have dramatic effects but not always. Man is not the only reason; natural invasions occur. Thus all three statements are incorrect. So (d) is correct.

subject: Reading Comprehension
question: Why does man introduce exotic species into new geographical areas? 1. To breed exotic species with local varieties. 2. To increase agricultural productivity. 3. For beautification and landscaping. Which of the above statements is/are correct?
explanation: The passage mentions introducing species to produce new agricultural products (increasing productivity) and for recreational opportunities (which can include beautification/landscaping). It does not mention breeding exotic with local varieties. Thus statements 2 and 3 are correct, so (b).

subject: Reading Comprehension
question: How is homogenization prevented under natural conditions?

option.a: Evolution of groups of species specific to local habitats.
option.b: Presence of oceans and mountain ranges.
option.c: Strong adaptation of groups of species to local physical and climatic conditions.
option.d: All the statements (a), (b) and (c) given above are correct in this context.

explanation: The passage explains that biodiversity is maintained by centers of endemism (species specific to local habitats - a), physical barriers like oceans and mountains (b), and limited dispersal powers due to adaptation to local conditions (c). All three prevent homogenization, so (d) is correct.

subject: Reading Comprehension
question: How have the human beings influenced the biodiversity? 1. By smuggling live organisms. 2. By building highways. 3. By making ecosystems sensitive so that new species are not allowed. 4. By ensuring that new species do not have major impact on local species. Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: The passage mentions human actions like transport (including smuggling - 1) and building infrastructure (highways facilitate dispersal - 2). Statements 3 and 4 are opposite: humans often make ecosystems more vulnerable and introductions often have major impacts. Thus (a) 1 and 2 are correct.

subject: Reading Comprehension
question: What can be the impact of invasion of exotic species on an ecosystem? 1. Erosion of endemic species. 2. Change in the species composition of the community of the ecosystem. Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
explanation: The passage gives example of brown tree snake causing extinction of endemic birds (erosion of endemic species - 1). It also discusses homogenization, which changes species composition (2). Both impacts occur, so (c) is correct.

subject: Reading Comprehension
question: According to the passage, why is a serious tension perceived between democracy and development by the detractors of democracy?

option.a: Democracy and development are distinct and separate goals.
option.b: Economic growth can be promoted successfully without pursuing a democratic system of governance.
option.c: Non-democratic regimes deliver economic growth faster and far more successfully than democratic ones.
option.d: All the statements (a), (b) and (c) given above are correct in this context.

explanation: The passage says detractors see serious tensions between democracy and development, and they point to East Asian countries that promoted economic growth without pursuing democracy. Option (b) captures that. Option (a) is not the tension itself but a separate view. Option (c) is too strong and not explicitly stated. Thus (b) is correct.

subject: Reading Comprehension
question: According to the passage, what should be the ultimate assessment/aim/view of development?

option.a: Rise in the per capita income and industrial growth rates.
option.b: Improvement in the Human Development Index and GNP.
option.c: Rise in the savings and consumption trends.
option.d: Extent of real freedom that citizens enjoy.

explanation: The passage states that development cannot be divorced from the lives people can lead and the real freedom they enjoy. The ultimate aim is the extent of real freedom, not just income or GNP. Thus (d) is correct.

subject: Reading Comprehension
question: What does a 'constitutive' connection between democracy and development imply?

option.a: The relation between them has to be seen through external links.
option.b: Political and civil rights only can lead to economic development.
option.c: Political liberties and democratic rights are essential elements of development.
option.d: None of the statements (a), (b) and (c) given above is correct in this context.

explanation: The passage explains that political liberties and democratic rights are among the 'constituent components' of development, meaning they are essential elements, not just external links. Option (c) correctly states this. Option (a) is opposite; (b) says 'only' which is too strong. Hence (c).

subject: Reading Comprehension
question: With reference to the passage, consider the following statements: 1. It is desirable that the impact of Foreign Direct Investment should be pro-competitive. 2. The entry of foreign investors invariably leads to the inflated prices in domestic markets. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The passage (not fully shown in extract but typical UPSC context) suggests that pro-competitive impact is desirable. Statement 2 says 'invariably leads to inflated prices' which is false; FDI can lower prices. Thus only statement 1 is correct, so (a).

subject: Reading Comprehension
question: According to the passage, how does a foreign investor dominate the relevant domestic market? 1. Multinational companies get accustomed to domestic laws. 2. Foreign companies establish joint ventures with domestic companies. 3. Affiliates in a particular market/sector lose their independence as their parent companies overseas merge. 4. Foreign companies lower the cost of their products as compared to that of products of domestic companies. Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: Based on typical passage content (though not fully visible), statements 1,2,3 are common ways foreign investors dominate (adapting to laws, joint ventures, mergers reducing independence). Statement 4 (lowering costs) is a competitive advantage but not necessarily domination; also could be pro-competitive. The passage likely excludes 4. Hence (c) is correct.

subject: Reading Comprehension
question: What is the inference from this passage?

option.a: Foreign investors and multinational companies always dominate the domestic market.
option.b: It is not in the best interests of the domestic economy to allow mergers of companies.
option.c: With competition law, it is easy to ensure a level playing field between domestic and foreign firms.
option.d: For countries with open economy, Foreign Direct Investment is essential for growth.

explanation: The passage likely discusses the need for competition law to prevent anti-competitive behavior by foreign investors. Option (c) is a reasonable inference. Options (a) and (d) are too absolute; (b) is not stated. Thus (c).

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Examine the following statements: 1. I watch TV only if I am bored. 2. I am never bored when I have my brother's company. 3. Whenever I go to the theatre I take my brother along. Which one of the following conclusions is valid in the context of the above statements?

option.a: If I am bored, I watch TV.
option.b: If I am bored, I seek my brother's company.
option.c: If I am not with my brother, then I watch TV.
option.d: If I am not bored, I do not watch TV.

explanation: Statement 1: 'I watch TV only if I am bored' means watching TV implies boredom. Contrapositive: If not bored, then not watch TV. Option (d) matches. Option (a) is the converse, not necessarily true (boredom may not lead to TV). Option (c) is not supported. Thus (d) is valid.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Only six roads A, B, C, P, Q and R connect a military camp to the rest of the country. Only one out of A, P and R is open at any one time. If B is closed, so is Q. Only one of A and B is open during storms. P is closed during floods. In this context, which one of the following statements is correct?

option.a: Under normal conditions only three roads are open.
option.b: During storms at least one road is open.
option.c: During floods only three roads are open.
option.d: During calamities all roads are closed.

explanation: Analyze: During storms, only one of A and B is open. Additionally, from 'only one out of A,P,R is open at any one time', during storms if A is open then P,R closed; if B is open then A closed, but P and R? Since 'only one of A,P,R open' means exactly one of those three is open at any time. During storms, either A or B is open. If A is open, then P,R closed, B? B can be closed (since only one of A,B open). That gives one road open (A). If B is open, then A closed, and since only one of A,P,R open, and A is closed, then either P or R could be open? However 'only one of A and B is open during storms' means if B open then A closed, but it doesn't restrict P,R. But the first condition 'only one out of A,P,R is open at any one time' applies always. So if B is open, then among A,P,R, exactly one must be open. Since A is closed, either P or R is open. That would give two roads open (B and one of P/R). But is that allowed? The condition 'only one of A and B is open during storms' - that is satisfied. However, we also have 'If B is closed, so is Q' - but B open so no info. So during storms, at least one road is open (either A or B, plus possibly P/R). Thus (b) is correct. (a) normal conditions unknown. (c) floods: P closed, but others? Not necessarily only three. (d) false. Hence (b).

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Examine the following statements: 1. None but students are members of the club. 2. Some members of the club are married persons. 3. All married persons are invited for dance. Which one of the following conclusions can be drawn from the above statements?

option.a: All students are invited for dance.
option.b: All married students of the club are invited for dance.
option.c: All members of the club are married persons.
option.d: None of the above conclusions can be drawn.

explanation: Statement 1: 'None but students are members' means only students can be members, i.e., all members are students. Statement 2: Some members are married, so some students (who are members) are married. Statement 3: All married persons are invited. Therefore, those married students who are members are invited. Option (b) says 'All married students of the club are invited for dance' - that is correct because they are married persons. Option (a) is too broad (all students, not just members). Option (c) is false. Thus (b) follows.

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Four political parties W, X, Y and Z decided to set up a joint candidate for the coming parliamentary elections. The formula agreed by them was the acceptance of a candidate by most of the parties. Four aspiring candidates, A, B, C and D approached the parties for their tickets. A was acceptable to W but not to Z. B was acceptable to Y but not to X. C was acceptable to W and Y. D was acceptable to W and X. When candidate B was preferred by W and Z, candidate C was preferred by X and Z, and candidate A was acceptable to X but not to Y; who got the ticket?
explanation: We need to determine final acceptabilities. Initially: A: W yes, Z no, X?, Y?; B: Y yes, X no, W?, Z?; C: W yes, Y yes, X?, Z?; D: W yes, X yes, Y?, Z?. Then additional info: 'When candidate B was preferred by W and Z' means B becomes acceptable to W and Z as well. So B now: Y yes, X no, W yes, Z yes. 'Candidate C was preferred by X and Z' means C acceptable to X and Z. So C: W yes, Y yes, X yes, Z yes (all four). 'Candidate A was acceptable to X but not to Y' means A: X yes, Y no, and earlier W yes, Z no. So A: W yes, X yes, Y no, Z no. D remains: W yes, X yes, Y?, Z? (no info on Y and Z for D). Now most of parties means at least 3 out of 4. Candidate A has 2 (W,X). Candidate B has 3 (W,Y,Z) - X no, so 3. Candidate C has 4. Candidate D has at least 2 (W,X), unknown Y,Z. Since C has 4, C is accepted by most (all). So C gets the ticket. Thus answer (c).

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Consider the following statements : 1. All X-brand cars parked here are white. 2. Some of them have radial tyres. 3. All X-brand cars manufactured after 1986 have radial tyres. 4. All cars are not X-brand. Which one of the following conclusions can be drawn from the above statements ?

option.a: Only white cars are parked here.
option.b: Some white X-brand cars with radial tyres are parked here.
option.c: Cars other than X-brand cannot have radial tyres.
option.d: Most of the X-brand cars are manufactured before 1986.

explanation: From (1) all X-brand cars parked are white. (2) Some of them have radial tyres. 'Them' likely refers to 'X-brand cars' from statement 1. So statement 2 means: Some X-brand cars (parked here) have radial tyres. Then from 1, those are white. So we can conclude that some white X-brand cars with radial tyres are parked here. That makes (b) valid. Option (a) false; (c) false; (d) cannot be inferred. Thus answer (b).

subject: Logical Reasoning
question: Consider the following statement : The Third World War, if it ever starts, will end very quickly with the possible end of civilization. It is only the misuse of nuclear power which will trigger it. Based on the above statement, which one of the following inferences is correct ?

option.a: Nuclear power will be used in the Third World War.
option.b: There will be no civilization left after the Third World War.
option.c: The growth of nuclear power will destroy civilization in the long run.
option.d: The Third World War will not take place.

explanation: The statement says the war will be triggered only by misuse of nuclear power, and will end quickly with possible end of civilization. Thus if it starts, nuclear power will be used (since misuse triggers it). Option (a) is a valid inference. Option (b) says 'no civilization left' - the statement says 'possible', not certain. Option (c) talks about growth of nuclear power, not directly. Option (d) is not stated. Hence (a).

subject: Data Interpretation
question: Consider the following information regarding the performance of a class of 1000 students in four different tests : Test I II III IV Average marks 60 60 70 80 Range of marks 20 to 90 30 to 75 45 to 100 20 to 100. If a student scores 74 marks in one of the four tests, in which one of the following tests is her performance the best comparatively ?
explanation: To determine comparative performance, we need to see how 74 relates to the distribution. Typically, using z-score or percentile. Given average and range. Test I: avg 60, range 20-90. 74 is 14 above avg, max 90. Test II: avg 60, range 30-75. 74 is 14 above avg, but max is 75, so 74 is near top. Test III: avg 70, range 45-100. 74 is only 4 above avg. Test IV: avg 80, range 20-100. 74 is below avg. So relative to the class, in Test II, 74 is very high (close to maximum), while in others it's less impressive. Thus best performance is in Test II. Answer (b).

subject: Reading Comprehension
question: Agricultural collectivities such as group based farming can provide the rural poor 1. empowerment. 2. increased agricultural productivity. 3. safeguard against exploitative markets. 4. surplus production of agricultural commodities. Select the correct answer using the codes given below :
explanation: From Passage-1 (page 17): The passage states that group approach offers scope for poverty alleviation, empowering the poor (1), enhancing agricultural productivity (2), and as protection against free market individualism (safeguard against exploitative markets - 3). Surplus production (4) is not explicitly mentioned as a direct benefit; productivity increase may lead to surplus but not stated. Thus 1,2,3 are correct, so answer (b).

subject: Reading Comprehension
question: What does the author imply by 'gender impact'?

option.a: Women are doubtful participants in cooperatives.
option.b: Family cooperatives may not include women.
option.d: Women's role in transition economies is highly restrictive.

explanation: The passage says 'although the gender impact of the family cooperatives in the transition economies are uncertain, the Indian examples of women-only groups farming offer considerable potential for benefiting women.' Thus gender impact refers to how women benefit (or not) from group farming. Option (c) captures this. Other options are not directly implied. Hence (c).

subject: Reading Comprehension
question: Consider the following assumptions : 1. It is imperative for transition economies to have agricultural collectivities. 2. Agricultural productivity can be increased by group approach to farming. With reference to the above passage, which of these assumptions is/are valid ?
explanation: The passage says group approach offers scope for enhancing agricultural productivity, so assumption 2 is valid. However, it does not say it is 'imperative' for transition economies; it gives examples but not necessity. Thus only assumption 2 is valid, answer (b).

subject: Reading Comprehension
question: According to the passage, what does 'deepening of democracy' mean in the Western context?

option.a: Consolidation of group and class identities.
option.b: Democratization translated as greater involvement of people.
option.c: Democratization as greater involvement of 'atomized' individuals in the public sphere.
option.d: None of the statements (a), (b) and (c) given above is correct in this context.

explanation: The passage states: 'In a typical Western liberal context, deepening of democracy invariably leads to consolidation of liberal values... individuals are getting involved in the public sphere not as 'atomized' individuals...' Actually the passage contrasts Western context where deepening democracy means involvement of atomized individuals? Let's read: 'In a typical Western liberal context, deepening of democracy invariably leads to consolidation of liberal values. In the Indian context, democratization is translated into greater involvement of people not as 'individuals' which is a staple to liberal discourse, but as communities or groups.' So in Western context, deepening democracy involves individuals (atomized). Thus option (c) is correct. (a) and (b) are Indian context. Hence (c).

2. Union Ministry of Home Affairs decides the election schedule for the conduct of both general elections and bye-elections.
3. Election Commission resolves the disputes relating to splits/mergers of recognised political parties.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Only statement 3 is correct. ECI resolves disputes about party splits.

subject: General Studies
question:
Consider the following statements:
1. In the election for Lok Sabha or State Assembly, the winning candidate must get at least 50 percent of the votes polled, to be declared elected.
2. According to the provisions laid down in the Constitution of India, in Lok Sabha, the Speaker's post goes to the majority party and the Deputy Speaker's to the Opposition.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Both statements are incorrect. Simple majority wins and Speaker/Deputy Speaker posts not reserved.

subject: General Studies
question:
Which of the following has/have occurred in India after its liberalization of economic policies in 1991?
1. Share of agriculture in GDP increased enormously.
2. Share of India's exports in world trade increased.
3. FDI inflows increased.
4. India's foreign exchange reserves increased enormously.
Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
explanation: Statements 2, 3 and 4 occurred after 1991 liberalization.

subject: General Studies
question: What is the application of Somatic Cell Nuclear Transfer Technology?

option.d: Production of organisms free of diseases

explanation: Somatic Cell Nuclear Transfer is used for reproductive cloning of animals.

subject: General Studies
question:
Consider the following statements:
1. National Payments Corporation of India (NPCI) helps in promoting the financial inclusion in the country.
2. NPCI has launched RuPay, a card payment scheme.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Both statements are correct about NPCI.

subject: General Studies
question: The term 'M-STrIPES' is sometimes seen in the news in the context of
explanation: M-STrIPES is a monitoring system for tiger reserves.

subject: General Studies
question:
What is/are the most likely advantages of implementing 'Goods and Services Tax (GST)'?
1. It will replace multiple taxes collected by multiple authorities and will thus create a single market in India.
2. It will drastically reduce the 'Current Account Deficit' of India and will enable it to increase its foreign exchange reserves.
3. It will enormously increase the growth and size of economy of India and will enable it to overtake China in the near future.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation: Only statement 1 is a likely advantage of GST implementation.

subject: General Studies
question: 'Broad-based Trade and Investment Agreement (BTIA)' is sometimes seen in the news in the context of negotiations held between India and

option.c: Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development

explanation: BTIA refers to negotiations between India and European Union.

subject: General Studies
question:
Consider the following statements:
1. India has ratified the Trade Facilitation Agreement (TFA) of WTO.
2. TFA is a part of WTO's Bali Ministerial Package of 2013.
3. TFA came into force in January 2016.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: All three statements are correct about TFA.

subject: General Studies
question: What is the importance of developing Chabahar Port by India?

option.a: India's trade with African countries will enormously increase.
option.b: India's relations with oil-producing Arab countries will be strengthened.
option.c: India will not depend on Pakistan for access to Afghanistan and Central Asia.
option.d: Pakistan will facilitate and protect the installation of a gas pipeline between Iraq and India.

explanation: Chabahar Port provides India access to Afghanistan and Central Asia without Pakistan dependency.

subject: General Studies
question: In India, it is legally mandatory for which of the following to report on cyber security incidents?
explanation: All three - service providers, data centres and body corporate must report cyber security incidents.

subject: General Studies
question: Right to vote and to be elected in India is a
explanation: Right to vote and be elected is a Constitutional Right in India.

subject: General Studies
question: What is the purpose of 'evolved Laser Interferometer Space Antenna (eLISA)' project?

option.c: To detect the effectiveness of missile defence system
option.d: To study the effect of solar flares on our communication systems

explanation: eLISA project aims to detect gravitational waves in space.

subject: General Studies
question: What is the purpose of 'Vidyanjali Yojana'?

option.a: To enable the famous foreign educational institutions to open their campuses in India.
option.b: To increase the quality of education provided in government schools by taking help from the private sector and the community.
option.c: To encourage voluntary monetary contributions from private individuals and organizations so as to improve the infrastructure facilities for primary and secondary schools.

explanation: Vidyanjali aims to improve government school education through private sector and community help.

subject: General Studies
question: What is the aim of the programme 'Unnat Bharat Abhiyan'?

option.a: Achieving 100% literacy by promoting collaboration between voluntary organizations and government's education system and local communities.
option.b: Connecting institutions of higher education with local communities to address development challenges through appropriate technologies.
option.c: Strengthening India's scientific research institutions in order to make India a scientific and technological power.
option.d: Developing human capital by allocating special funds for health care and education of rural and urban poor, and organizing skill development programmes and vocational training for them.

explanation: Unnat Bharat Abhiyan connects higher education institutions with communities to address development challenges.

subject: General Studies
question:
